title: Whether minor sports leagues should resist televising matches
summary: Argue whether a small regional sports league should sell television rights and grow its audience, or refuse the cameras to protect the live atmosphere and the small clubs that depend on it.
topic: Whether minor sports leagues should resist televising matches
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether a minor regional sports league — Vietnamese V.League 2, Argentine Primera B, a Polish handball division — should sell television rights to grow its audience, or refuse the cameras to protect the live atmosphere, the small clubs that depend on gate revenue, and the local character that disappears once games are watched mostly through a screen. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Avoid nostalgic broad strokes about 'the spirit of the game'.
hintStarters: Walking into a half-empty handball arena in Lublin on a Friday night, you notice that the locals who do show up know each other by first name, and that the visiting team's coach…
What a minor sports league actually sells, when it sells television rights, is something the league's own administrators rarely fully understand until…
Granted, the case for refusing television revenue can be made to sound like sentimental small-club romanticism, and the modest budgets of regional clubs make the argument hard to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cơ sở người hâm mộ trung thành
thương mại hóa thể thao địa phương
chất lượng sản xuất truyền hình
chạy theo số lượng khán giả
khán đài không thể quản lý
giọng vùng miền trên khán đài

sampleText:
Walking into a half-empty handball arena in Lublin on a Friday night, you notice that the locals who do show up know each other by first name, and that the visiting team's coach is having a public argument with a man in row four who, it turns out, is the local pharmacist. The match is loud, badly officiated, and undeniably alive. None of this would survive contact with a national broadcaster, which is why the question of selling the league's television rights, when it finally arrives at the next board meeting, is not the easy yes the bigger clubs assume.

What a minor sports league actually sells, when it sells television rights, is something the league's own administrators rarely fully understand until the contracts are signed. They believe they are selling the games. They are also selling the kick-off times — Saturday two o'clock becomes Sunday seven in the evening — and the right to dictate which matches are picked, the camera angles that determine what counts as a moment, and, in the long run, the rhythm of the league's own season. A broadcaster does not simply show what is there. It quietly reshapes what is shown.

Granted, the case for refusing television revenue can be made to sound like sentimental small-club romanticism, and the modest budgets of regional clubs make the argument hard to defend at a board meeting where the alternative is unpaid bills. A broadcast deal can keep three lower-division clubs solvent for a decade. Refusing it on principle in the name of atmosphere can come across as the position of comfortable spectators who do not depend on the league for their wages. Money is not a vulgar consideration when wages are owed.

Even so, the bigger leagues that have already gone through this transition do not offer reassuring evidence. Once matches are rescheduled for broadcast slots, attendance begins a slow drift downward that is rarely arrested. Once production values are introduced, the rough, local character of the commentary, the kit, and the small advertising boards begins to feel embarrassing on television, and is gradually replaced. Within a decade, the league looks like a smaller version of the top one — without the spending power to actually compete, and without the local texture that was its only real distinction.

The honest verdict is the awkward middle. A minor league can sell a small share of television rights — one televised game a week, no fixture reshuffling, no production override — provided the contract names what cannot be moved. What it must not sell is the right to reshape its own season. The moment that goes, what the league is selling is no longer its games. It is the long, quiet permission to stop being what made it worth broadcasting in the first place.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose what is really being sold
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a minor sports league actually sells, when it sells television rights, is something the league's own administrators rarely fully understand' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' với một mệnh đề thời gian chèn vào ('when it sells television rights') để tách câu hỏi và câu trả lời, tạo nhịp điệu chậm có chủ đích. 'Actually' báo hiệu rằng câu trả lời sắp tới sẽ bất ngờ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a minor sports league actually sells, when it sells television rights, is something the league's own administrators rarely fully understand' uses a what-cleft with an inserted temporal clause ('when it sells television rights') that separates the question and answer, creating a deliberate slow rhythm. 'Actually' signals that the answer about to come will be unexpected.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive 'is shown' / 'is rescheduled' to background the broadcaster's agency
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It quietly reshapes what is shown' và 'Once matches are rescheduled for broadcast slots, attendance begins a slow drift downward that is rarely arrested' dùng passive ('what is shown', 'are rescheduled', 'is rarely arrested') để mô tả một quá trình mà người chịu trách nhiệm (đài truyền hình) không bao giờ được đặt vào vị trí chủ ngữ. Đây là cách mô tả ảnh hưởng có hệ thống mà không cần ngón tay chỉ trỏ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It quietly reshapes what is shown' and 'Once matches are rescheduled for broadcast slots, attendance begins a slow drift downward that is rarely arrested' use the passive ('what is shown', 'are rescheduled', 'is rarely arrested') to describe a process in which the responsible party (the broadcaster) is never placed in subject position. This is a way to describe systemic influence without an accusing finger.

sampleTranslationVi:
Đi vào một sân vận động bóng ném vắng một nửa ở Lublin vào một tối thứ Sáu, bạn nhận ra rằng những người dân địa phương đến đều biết nhau bằng tên riêng, và rằng huấn luyện viên đội khách đang có một cuộc tranh cãi công khai với một người đàn ông ở hàng bốn mà, hóa ra, là dược sĩ địa phương. Trận đấu thì ồn ào, trọng tài kém, và không thể phủ nhận là sống động. Không điều nào trong số này sẽ sống sót khi tiếp xúc với một đài truyền hình quốc gia, đó là lý do tại sao câu hỏi bán bản quyền truyền hình của giải, khi cuối cùng nó đến cuộc họp hội đồng tiếp theo, không phải là cái 'có' dễ dàng mà các câu lạc bộ lớn hơn giả định.

Điều mà một giải đấu thể thao hạng dưới thực sự bán, khi nó bán bản quyền truyền hình, là điều mà chính các quản trị viên của giải hiếm khi hiểu đầy đủ cho đến khi các hợp đồng đã được ký. Họ tin rằng họ đang bán các trận đấu. Họ cũng đang bán giờ bóng lăn — thứ Bảy hai giờ chiều trở thành Chủ Nhật bảy giờ tối — và quyền quyết định trận nào được chọn, các góc máy quyết định cái gì được tính là khoảnh khắc, và về lâu dài, nhịp điệu của chính mùa giải. Một đài truyền hình không đơn giản là chiếu cái có sẵn. Nó lặng lẽ định hình lại cái được chiếu.

Công nhận, lập luận từ chối doanh thu truyền hình có thể bị làm cho nghe giống như chủ nghĩa lãng mạn câu lạc bộ nhỏ ủy mị, và ngân sách khiêm tốn của các câu lạc bộ vùng làm cho lập luận đó khó bảo vệ tại một cuộc họp hội đồng nơi lựa chọn thay thế là hóa đơn chưa thanh toán. Một thỏa thuận phát sóng có thể giữ ba câu lạc bộ hạng dưới khả năng thanh toán trong một thập kỷ. Từ chối nó trên nguyên tắc nhân danh không khí có thể bị xem như lập trường của những khán giả thoải mái không phụ thuộc vào giải để kiếm lương. Tiền không phải là một cân nhắc thô tục khi lương đang nợ.

Dẫu vậy, các giải đấu lớn hơn đã đi qua quá trình chuyển đổi này không cung cấp bằng chứng yên tâm. Một khi các trận đấu được đổi lịch cho các khung phát sóng, lượng khán giả bắt đầu trôi dạt xuống chậm rãi hiếm khi được chặn lại. Một khi giá trị sản xuất được giới thiệu, tính cách thô mộc, địa phương của bình luận, trang phục thi đấu, và các bảng quảng cáo nhỏ bắt đầu cảm thấy xấu hổ trên truyền hình, và dần dần bị thay thế. Trong vòng một thập kỷ, giải đấu trông giống như một phiên bản nhỏ hơn của giải hàng đầu — không có sức mua để thực sự cạnh tranh, và không có kết cấu địa phương vốn là sự phân biệt thực sự duy nhất của nó.

Phán quyết trung thực là cái ở giữa khó xử. Một giải đấu hạng dưới có thể bán một phần nhỏ bản quyền truyền hình — một trận được phát mỗi tuần, không sắp xếp lại lịch, không can thiệp sản xuất — miễn là hợp đồng nêu rõ những gì không thể di chuyển. Điều nó không được bán là quyền định hình lại mùa giải của chính nó. Khoảnh khắc đó mất đi, cái mà giải đấu đang bán không còn là các trận đấu của nó nữa. Đó là sự cho phép dài, lặng lẽ để ngừng là cái đã làm cho nó đáng được phát sóng ngay từ đầu.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose what is really being sold with TV rights?
options: What a minor sports league actually sells, when it sells television rights, is something the league's own administrators rarely fully understand until the contracts are signed.
Television rights sells what a minor sports league actually when it is something the league's own administrators rarely fully understand the contracts are signed until.
The contracts are signed until what a minor sports league actually sells when it sells television rights administrators rarely fully understand is something the league's own.
Is something administrators what a minor sports league actually sells the league's own rarely fully understand when it sells television rights until the contracts are signed.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' with an inserted temporal clause creates a deliberate slow rhythm before delivering the diagnosis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with 'when it sells…' in the middle.

options: Fans who are required to bring screens to matches
A fandom that experiences the sport mostly through television or streaming, with less or no physical presence at the stadium
Fans recruited through screen advertising
Fans who watch only on cinema screens
mcQuestions.explanation: Screen-mediated fandom is the kind that grows when matches are watched rather than attended — and the essay argues this kind of fandom changes what the sport is.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'mediated' do to a relationship? Apply that to fandom.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted,' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the case for refusing television revenue can be made to sound like sentimental small-club romanticism.
Television revenue granted the case for refusing can be made to sound like sentimental small-club romanticism.
Refusing television granted revenue the case for can be made to sound romanticism like sentimental small-club.
Sentimental small-club romanticism the case can be made for refusing television revenue granted to sound like.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction places matches in subject position to background the broadcaster?
options: Once matches are rescheduled for broadcast slots, attendance begins a slow drift downward that is rarely arrested.
Broadcast slots once attendance begins matches are rescheduled for a slow drift downward that is rarely arrested.
Rarely arrested a slow drift downward attendance begins matches are rescheduled once for broadcast slots.
Attendance begins a slow drift downward once for broadcast slots that is rarely arrested matches are rescheduled.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'are rescheduled' and 'is rarely arrested' are passive constructions that put matches and attendance in subject position, hiding the broadcaster as the actor.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'are rescheduled' and 'is rarely arrested'?

options: Repainting the stadium furniture
Moving scheduled matches to different days or times — usually to suit broadcasters rather than supporters
Reordering the names of players on the team list
Cancelling matches
mcQuestions.explanation: Fixtures are scheduled matches. Reshuffling them for broadcast moves the kick-off times away from the supporters' weekly habit, which the essay identifies as one cost of TV deals.
mcQuestions.hint: What is a fixture, and what is reshuffling?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Walking into a half-empty handball arena in Lublin on a Friday night, you notice that the locals who do show up know each other by first name.
You notice walking into a half-empty handball arena in Lublin on a Friday night that the locals who do show up know each other by first name.
The locals who do show up know each other by first name walking into a half-empty handball arena in Lublin on a Friday night you notice.
On a Friday night in a half-empty handball arena in Lublin walking into you notice the locals who do show up know each other by first name that.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Walking into a half-empty handball arena in Lublin on a Friday night' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all TV deals are bad.
Even so, the bigger leagues that have already gone through this transition do not offer reassuring evidence.
On the contrary, no league has ever signed a TV deal.
Furthermore, all broadcasters are heroic.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (wages must be paid) and pivots back to the writer's main argument using counter-evidence from leagues that have already gone through the change.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'production values' mean in sports broadcasting?
options: The values printed on tickets
The technical and aesthetic quality of how a broadcast is made — cameras, graphics, commentary, lighting
The financial value of the production company
The values of the production team
mcQuestions.explanation: Production values shape how the sport looks on screen — and the essay argues their introduction quietly erodes the league's rough local character.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'production' refer to in broadcasting, and what kind of 'values' are at stake?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, selling TV rights is just basically the death of minor leagues, no question.
It's kind of obvious that small clubs should stay off TV, full stop.
The acquisition of broadcast contracts by minor leagues has demonstrably reshaped scheduling, presentation, and supporter behaviour across multiple regional competitions in the past two decades.
TV is like, you know, supposed to ruin small sports and stuff.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('acquisition of broadcast contracts', 'demonstrably reshaped', 'regional competitions') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Minor leagues should sell every broadcast right they can at any price.
Minor leagues should refuse all television rights forever.

Minor leagues can sell a small, carefully constrained share of television rights — limited games, no rescheduling, no production override — but must refuse to sell the right to reshape the league's own season, or they will lose what made them broadcastable in the first place.
Minor leagues should disband if they cannot survive without TV money.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines a constrained middle position: limited TV exposure with hard contractual guarantees about scheduling and presentation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific verdict.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'a smaller version of the top one — without the spending power to actually compete'?
options: A league that has won the championship multiple times
A league that has copied the surface style of the top league but cannot afford the players to compete with it, losing its own identity in the process
A league exactly half the size of the top one
A league that has merged with the top one
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase names a recurring trap: imitating the visual style of the top league without the resources to compete at that level — a worst-of-both-worlds outcome.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to look like a top league without the budget to be one?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'gate revenue' mean for a sports club?
options: Money earned from selling stadium gates as scrap metal
Income from match-day ticket sales
Tax revenue collected at the city gates
Money paid to the stadium gatekeepers
mcQuestions.explanation: Gate revenue is what comes in through the turnstiles — the live attendance income that minor clubs disproportionately depend on, and that television deals can quietly erode.
mcQuestions.hint: What is 'the gate' in stadium language?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: The bigger leagues that have already gone through this transition do not offer reassuring evidence.
Have already gone through this transition the bigger leagues that do not offer reassuring evidence.
Reassuring evidence do not offer the bigger leagues that have already gone through this transition.
This transition the bigger leagues that have already gone through do not reassuring evidence offer.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that have already gone through this transition' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which bigger leagues the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'production override' mean as the essay uses it?
options: The right to skip production meetings
A contractual right granted to a broadcaster to overrule a league's preferences on how matches are presented — camera angles, commentary style, on-screen graphics
Override switches in the broadcast control room
Production work done in overtime
mcQuestions.explanation: When the broadcaster has production override, the league no longer controls how its games look — and the essay argues this is one of the rights minor leagues must retain.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'override' do to someone else's authority?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp, summary verdict?
options: In conclusion, TV deals are quite complicated for small leagues.
Therefore, all minor leagues must reject TV deals immediately.
It is the long, quiet permission to stop being what made it worth broadcasting in the first place.
To sum up, both selling and refusing TV rights have advantages.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp, paradoxical diagnosis: the rights being sold ultimately destroy the value that made them sellable.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a memorable paradox about what TV deals quietly do?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the most important right a minor league must NOT sell?
options: The right to print its own team shirts
The right to reshape its own season — its kick-off times, fixture calendar, and the presentation of its matches
The right to choose its own referees
The right to own its own stadium
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph five names exactly this: 'What it must not sell is the right to reshape its own season.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific list of what must not be sold.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a minor sports league or amateur competition you have actually watched in person. What about the live experience would not survive being televised? If you were on the board, what three clauses would you insist on in a broadcast contract before signing?
